title: Root operations (`--userns=auto`) behaving rootlessly in unprivileged LXC
body:
### Issue Description

When attempting to run containers as root using the `--userns=auto` option, the operations are seemingly carried out as though I am a rootless user. It does not look for ID mappings of a user named `container`, and it does not map IDs to the desired range.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Install podman in an unprivileged LXC.
2. `podman run --userns=auto busybox` (as root)
3. Add user and group ID mappings `root:100000:1000000`.
4. `podman run --userns=auto:size=512 busybox cat /proc/self/uid_map`

### Describe the results you received

Podman looks for ID mappings for the `root` user.
```
# podman run --userns=auto busybox
ERRO[0000] Cannot find mappings for user "root": no subuid ranges found for user "root" in /etc/subuid 
Error: creating container storage: not enough unused IDs in user namespace
```
After adding them (`root:100000:1000000`), containers are mapped starting at ID 1.
```
# podman run --userns=auto:size=512 busybox cat /proc/self/uid_map
         0          1        512
# podman run --userns=auto:size=512 busybox cat /proc/self/uid_map
         0        513        512
# podman run --userns=auto:size=512 busybox cat /proc/self/uid_map
         0       1025        512
```

### Describe the results you expected

Podman should look for ID mappings for the `containers` user, and map IDs starting at the given map range of 100000.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

Yes (LXC)

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

I have verified that it works as expected while running in a privileged LXC. I don't know what the crucial difference is. I notice that `podman info` does give me `rootless: false`.

comments.body:
So, as far as I can tell, the reason for the discrepancy with `podman info` is that, there, I am never considered rootless if my UID is 0, whereas when looking up ID mappings, the rootful check can still fail in other ways.

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/libpod/info_linux.go#L47
https://github.com/containers/storage/blob/main/userns.go#L30

I don't know which approach is more accurate, and I don't even know which check I am failing. I can see that `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` is absent in the list of capabilities, but the same is true in a privileged LXC. Either way, I would like to be considered root in this scenario.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
